summary:
The user, Analyst 3, started their shift to investigate a service outage previously narrowed down to a TLS certificate issue. The user focused on the Certificate Lifecycle Service (CLS), analyzing audit logs from the nightly certificate rotation job. They discovered the job had a "partial_success" status, with 119 of 312 certificates (38.1%) being issued with an incorrect Subject Alternative Name (SAN). This percentage precisely matched the proportion of failing pods identified in the prior shift.

The user confirmed this as the root cause by directly inspecting an affected pod's certificate, cross-referencing its serial number with earlier error logs, and running a quantitative test that showed a 38.1% mTLS failure rate. They established a full causal chain: bad certs led to mTLS failures, causing application threads to hang and exhaust the database connection pool. With high confidence, the user escalated the incident to SEV-0 and monitored the successful canary deployment of a fix. Before handing off, they raised two new red flags concerning the underlying code bug and the "soft-fail" configuration that allowed the incident to occur, successfully resolving all nine previous open items.
key_facts: The user investigated the Certificate Lifecycle Service (CLS) audit logs for the nightly rotation job.
The rotation job (`cls-rotate-20260315-0100`) had a `partial_success` status, with 119 of 312 certificates generating warnings.
The 119 warnings represented 38.1% of the batch, matching the percentage of failing pods from the previous shift.
The root cause was identified as an incorrect Subject Alternative Name (SAN) on the certificates (`*.mesh.internal` instead of `*.mesh.local`).
The faulty certificates were deployed because the CLS job was in a non-blocking "soft-fail mode".
The user confirmed the incorrect SAN and certificate serial number on an affected pod, matching it to prior error logs.
A quantitative mTLS handshake test produced a 38.1% failure rate, providing definitive proof.
The user established the full causal chain: bad certs -> mTLS failures -> application thread hangs -> DB connection pool exhaustion.
The incident was escalated from SEV-1 to SEV-0.
A remediation plan (`CHG-89104`) was initiated, and its canary phase was successful.
All 9 open red flags from previous shifts (A and B) were resolved.
Two new red flags (C-RF1, C-RF2) were created to investigate the specific code bug and the 'soft-fail' process failure.
positions_taken: The user expressed high confidence in the root cause identification after correlating the CLS audit logs with the observed failure rate.
The user decided to escalate the incident to SEV-0 due to the confirmed cause, long duration, and customer impact.
The user asserted that the healing of the database pool without direct intervention was the final proof that it was a secondary symptom.
The user stated that the "soft-fail" mode was a separate, contributing problem to be investigated by the next shift.
The user raised two new red flags (C-RF1, C-RF2) to guide the post-mortem investigation into process and code failures.
The user concluded that the entire diagnostic narrative was internally consistent and accounted for all observations from prior shifts.
positions_changed: The user's findings definitively overturned the Shift A hypothesis that DB pool exhaustion was the root cause, re-classifying it as a downstream symptom.
The user's work confirmed and specified the root cause for the Shift B hypothesis, moving it from a 'TLS certificate issue' to a 'CLS SAN mismatch from a faulty rotation job'.
